lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring farm management on a contract or fee basis, including production oversight, tenant and lease management, budgeting and accounting, crop marketing coordination, compliance, reporting, and arrangement of operating services for client farms.
lens.excluded_activities: Crop-producing farms and tenant farms, standalone harvesting, labor supply, cultivation, accounting, appraisal, brokerage, or consulting engagements without ongoing farm management, captive internal managers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Landowners, heirs, trusts, estates, and agricultural investors retain an external manager through renewable management contracts; fees may be fixed, per acre, or linked to rent or adjusted farm income, with ongoing reporting and local oversight.

a.bridge: O*NET describes agricultural managers across farms and ranches rather than the wage-weighted employee mix of contract farm-management firms, while two provider descriptions show the outsourced service basket but not task time. I map records, reports, accounting, budgets, scheduling, market analysis, compliance drafting, lease administration, data review, and routine communications to AI exposure, while retaining negotiation, client trust, field inspection, agronomic judgment, exception handling, and supervision as less substitutable. Provider pages may overrepresent full-service professional workflows, biasing exposure upward relative to small firms.
a.rationale: The occupation combines a large information-processing and coordination layer with consequential decisions and recurring physical inspection. Existing use of accounting, database, GIS, scheduling, ERP, office, and farm-management software makes the digital layer accessible to AI, but no task-time or wage-weighted industry study was found.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-linked time studies from NAICS 115116 firms that allocate employee hours and wages across reporting, accounting, lease administration, field inspection, agronomy, marketing, and client work, plus before-and-after AI workflow telemetry, would materially change the range.
caveats: O*NET covers agricultural managers broadly and is not weighted to this industry's occupation mix.
Provider descriptions establish tasks but not current automation, time shares, or wages.
The frozen labor-share input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization factor, so it carries stated vintage and basis uncertainty.

rho.rationale: Within five years, document intake, bookkeeping support, report generation, data reconciliation, scheduling, lease abstraction, market summaries, client updates, and decision preparation are implementable with human review. Fragmented farm data, seasonal exceptions, fiduciary expectations, agronomic variability, cybersecurity, employee adoption, and the need for accountable local judgment limit full realization.
rho.change_evidence: A multi-firm deployment study reporting adoption, utilization, error rates, review time, labor hours avoided, client retention, and payback for AI-assisted accounting, reporting, lease, agronomy, and communication workflows would materially move implementation.
caveats: No farm-management-industry AI adoption or realized labor-savings study was located.
Implementation should vary by crop, geography, data quality, client sophistication, and existing software stack.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.

e.bridge: The Census definition makes contract- or fee-based management the defining activity of NAICS 115116, and a large provider reports one-year management contracts with many multi-decade clients. I treat those facts as strong proxies that LMM firms in the correctly classified population provide repeat outsourced services, then discount for one-off engagements, miscoding, captive arrangements, customer concentration, and firms that cannot transfer independently. The leading-provider evidence may overstate recurrence for smaller firms.
e.rationale: Unlike production industries, this code is structurally aligned with the target lens: establishments always provide management and may arrange partial or complete farm operations. Renewable annual contracts and long customer tenures demonstrate a recurring model, while the lower bound allows for misclassification and nontransferable owner-centric practices.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 58 dataset-estimated LMM firms showing contract term, recurring-revenue share, customer concentration, captive status, normalized EBITDA, and reliance on the selling owner would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The Census definition is a classification rule, not an audit of each firm's revenue quality.
The renewal and tenure evidence comes from one large provider rather than an industry sample.
The frozen count of 58 LMM firms is margin-bridged and quality ESTIMATE.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22 percent of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I apply that intention rate to eligible LMM farm-management firms and widen for plan noncompletion, internal or family transfers, owner dependence, and agricultural-owner demographics. The cross-industry employer sample may misstate both the age profile and transferability of professional farm managers.
s5.rationale: General employer-business evidence supports a meaningful five-year transaction pool, but intentions are not completed qualifying transfers and local client relationships may be difficult to transfer without the principal.
s5.change_evidence: Business-broker and transaction-database records for NAICS 115116, matched ownership ages, succession plans, completed control transfers, closures, and post-sale client retention, would materially change the probability.
caveats: Gallup measures stated plans rather than completed control transfers.
Its sample spans industries and firm sizes rather than LMM farm-management firms.
Gifts, public offerings, and internal transfers in survey responses may not qualify under the lens.

q.bridge: A farm-management provider discloses per-acre plus net-income fees for custom operations and 5 to 7 percent of cash-rent income for cash-rent management, while another provider uses one-year contracts. I infer that non-hourly fees let the manager initially retain internal labor savings, but annual renewal, transparent performance, client bargaining, and net-income-linked formulas share some benefit. The disclosed structures are provider examples rather than an industry billing distribution and may bias retention in either direction.
q.rationale: Recurring fixed, per-acre, rent-linked, or income-linked fees are less directly tied to manager labor hours than time billing, supporting meaningful retention. However, clients reassess value annually and can demand better service or lower fees, especially when AI tools become widely available to competitors and landowners.
q.change_evidence: An anonymized contract sample across LMM firms showing fee formula, annual escalator, term, renewal, service scope, procurement process, churn, discounts, and post-automation repricing would materially change retention.
caveats: Published fees come from one regional provider and may not represent national or specialty-crop practices.
One-year contracts increase repricing exposure even when customer relationships persist for decades.
Benefits from improved farm output can flow to clients through fee formulas and are distinct from internal labor savings.

d5.bridge: USDA NASS measures the stock and ownership of rented farmland rather than five-year constant-price demand for professional management. Its 2024 TOTAL results show over 2.0 million landowners renting 348 million acres, 79 percent owned by non-farming landlords, with principal landlords averaging 69.2 years old. I translate the large, aging, non-operating owner base into stable-to-growing outsourced-management need, tempered because most acres are not professionally managed and acreage, ownership transfer, and service quantity are different constructs.
d5.rationale: A large non-operating landlord base, inherited assets, trusts, multi-generational ownership, and investor demand support durable oversight needs. Consolidation, direct tenant relationships, commodity stress, and digital self-service can offset growth; the range therefore centers on modest rather than rapid expansion.
d5.change_evidence: Annual managed-acre and client-count histories from national and regional firms, TOTAL tabulations of professional-manager use, new-client origin and churn cohorts, and five-year landowner outsourcing-intention surveys would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Rented acres and landlord counts are an addressable-base proxy, not observed management-service demand.
The industry serves crops and geographies unevenly, while TOTAL covers all US farmland.
Farmland ownership transfers can create or end management mandates depending on the buyer.

o.rationale: Software can automate reporting, monitoring, analysis, and routine administration, and some landowners may self-serve. Most of the current basket still requires an accountable agent to inspect land, supervise operators, negotiate leases, handle exceptions, exercise agronomic and fiduciary judgment, and maintain local relationships, so operator displacement should be limited within five years.
o.change_evidence: Observed churn to software-only platforms, institutional insourcing rates, autonomous field-inspection performance, customer willingness-to-pay studies, and evidence of fully self-served lease and production oversight would materially lower this value.
caveats: The estimate separates operator requirement from labor substitution inside the operator.
Smaller cash-rent portfolios may be more self-serviceable than complex crop-share or specialty operations.
Remote sensing reduces visit frequency but does not necessarily remove accountability or field verification.

narrative.executive_view: Farm management services fit the recurring outsourced-service lens unusually well because contract- or fee-based management defines the industry. The work has a sizable digital layer and durable landowner need, but success depends on preserving local expertise, client trust, and accountable field judgment while automating administration and analysis.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can materially assist accounting, document intake, budgeting, reporting, lease administration, scheduling, market and yield analysis, compliance drafting, data reconciliation, and routine client updates. Managers still need to inspect farms, select and supervise operators, negotiate, resolve exceptions, and accept responsibility for consequential agronomic and financial decisions.
narrative.value_capture: Per-acre, fixed, rent-linked, and income-linked fees are not simple hourly billing, so internal labor savings can be retained initially. One-year renewals and transparent performance create recurring repricing pressure, while widely available tools can lower client willingness to pay for undifferentiated administrative work.
narrative.firm_availability: Correctly classified LMM firms should mostly be eligible recurring service providers, and the frozen dataset estimates 58 firms in band. General employer-owner evidence suggests meaningful five-year transfer intentions, but completed control deals, owner dependence, and post-sale retention remain unmeasured for this niche.
narrative.demand_durability: USDA reports a large rented-farmland base dominated by non-farming landlords, with an old principal-landlord population. Those conditions support continuing demand for professional oversight, although direct tenant relationships, consolidation, insourcing, and software self-service can constrain quantity growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Material risks include concentration by client, crop, and geography; annual contract renewal; dependence on individual managers; agronomic and fiduciary errors; weather and commodity stress; fragmented data; cybersecurity; customer self-service; institutional insourcing; and the absence of industry-specific task-time, AI adoption, transaction, churn, and managed-acre data.
narrative.principal_driver: A definitionally recurring contract model combines a large information-processing workload with a durable base of non-operating and aging farmland owners.
narrative.principal_weakness: Local relationships, site judgment, and principal dependence can cap implementation and make customer retention fragile through a control transfer.

sources.title: 2012 NAICS Definition File: 115116 Farm Management Services
sources.supports: Defines establishments as providing farm management on a contract or fee basis, always including management and potentially arranging partial or complete farm operations; supports the lens and e.

sources.title: 11-9013.00 - Farmers, Ranchers, and Other Agricultural Managers
sources.supports: Lists management, records, clerical coordination, marketing, analysis, compliance, supervision, inspection, negotiation, planning, scheduling, and relevant software; supports the task-mix proxy for a.

sources.title: Farm Management Services for Landowners
sources.supports: Describes recurring full-service workflows, regular reporting and visits, one-year contracts, and many multi-decade client relationships; supports a, e, q, and d5.

sources.title: Professional Farm Management: Preserving Your Legacy, Maximizing Your Land's Potential
sources.supports: Describes lease and tenant oversight, financial reporting, site visits, landowner segments, precision agriculture, and data-driven decisions; supports a and d5.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports US employer-owner age and five-year sell-or-transfer intentions; supports the s5 proxy.

sources.title: Most of the U.S. Rented Farmland is Owned by Non-Farmers
sources.supports: Reports the 2024 TOTAL survey's rented acres, landowner population, non-farming ownership share, rental economics, expected transfers, and landlord ages; supports d5.

sources.title: Unique Farm Management Fee System
sources.supports: Discloses per-acre, adjusted-net-income, and 5-to-7-percent cash-rent management fee structures; supports the q proxy.
